Maths - Number and operations.skill: Convert between place values
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between place values for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.2(A)). Students should understand that each place value position is 10 times the value of the position to its right and 1/10 the value of the position to its left. Key concepts: moving one place to the left multiplies the value by 10; moving one place to the right divides the value by 10; this pattern extends through both whole number and decimal places; understanding these relationships helps with multiplication and division by powers of 10. Vocabulary: place value, ten times, one-tenth, multiply, divide, position, digit, relationship, pattern, power of ten. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Place value review
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers place value fundamentals for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.2(B)). Students should understand how digits in a number represent values based on their position. Key concepts: each position in a number has a specific value (ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, etc.); the value of a digit equals the digit multiplied by its place value; numbers can be represented in standard form, expanded form, and word form; understanding place value is foundational for all number operations. Vocabulary: place value, digit, value, position, ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, standard form, expanded form, word form. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers lines of symmetry for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(B)). Students should practice organizing data sets into stem-and-leaf plots accurately. Students should accurately count and record data, choose appropriate categories or intervals, and verify that the total of all frequencies equals the number of data points. Problems should involve drawing conclusions from line plots appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Data analysis.prompt: This topic covers creating line plots with fractions for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(B)). Students should understand how to organize fractional data on a number line using X marks or dots to create a line plot. Key concepts: line plots display data along a number line; each data point is represented by a mark above the corresponding value; when data involves fractions, the number line must include fractional increments (halves, quarters, eighths); data should be sorted and counted before plotting. Students should choose appropriate scales with fractional values and accurately place data points. Vocabulary: line plot, data, fraction, number line, frequency, scale, increment, mark, value, half, quarter, eighth. Common errors include using incorrect scales, miscounting data points, or placing marks at wrong positions. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting frequency charts for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(B)). Students should understand how to read and analyze data from frequency charts to answer questions and draw conclusions. Key concepts: reading frequencies for specific categories; comparing frequencies between categories; finding the total number of data points by adding all frequencies; identifying the most and least frequent categories; using frequency data to make comparisons and solve multi-step problems. Vocabulary: frequency, chart, table, category, interpret, compare, total, most frequent, least frequent, data, analyze, conclusion. Problems should involve answering questions from frequency charts with real-world contexts appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers interpreting stem-and-leaf plots for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(B)). Students should understand how to read and analyze data from stem-and-leaf plots to answer questions. Key concepts: reading individual values by combining the stem and leaf; finding the minimum and maximum values; determining the range, median, and mode from the plot; counting the total number of data points; identifying clusters, gaps, and outliers in the distribution. Vocabulary: stem-and-leaf plot, stem, leaf, key, median, mode, range, minimum, maximum, cluster, gap, outlier, distribution, interpret, analyze. Students should practice extracting information and making comparisons from stem-and-leaf plots. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Data analysis.skill: Interpret frequency charts with numerical data
Maths - Data analysis.prompt: This topic covers interpreting frequency charts for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(B)). Students should understand how to read and analyze data from frequency charts to answer questions and draw conclusions. Key concepts: reading frequencies for specific categories; comparing frequencies between categories; finding the total number of data points by adding all frequencies; identifying the most and least frequent categories; using frequency data to make comparisons and solve multi-step problems. Vocabulary: frequency, chart, table, category, interpret, compare, total, most frequent, least frequent, data, analyze, conclusion. Problems should involve answering questions from frequency charts with real-world contexts appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers identifying fixed and variable expenses for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(A)). Students should understand the difference between fixed expenses (costs that stay the same each month, like rent or mortgage) and variable expenses (costs that change from month to month, like groceries or entertainment). Key concepts: fixed expenses are predictable and easier to budget for; variable expenses require estimation and tracking; both types must be accounted for in a personal budget; understanding which expenses are fixed versus variable helps with financial planning. Vocabulary: fixed expense, variable expense, budget, income, cost, monthly, rent, mortgage, utilities, groceries, entertainment, insurance, subscription. Students should practice categorizing common household expenses and understanding why this distinction matters for budgeting. Problems should involve realistic scenarios appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers calculating profit for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(B)). Students should understand that profit is the amount of money earned after subtracting all costs from total revenue. Key concepts: profit = revenue - expenses; revenue is the total money received from selling goods or services; expenses include the cost of materials, labor, and other business costs; a positive result means profit while a negative result means a loss. Students should apply arithmetic operations to calculate profit in word problems involving buying and selling scenarios. Vocabulary: profit, loss, revenue, income, expense, cost, sell, buy, earn, business, goods, services, investment. Problems should involve age-appropriate business scenarios like lemonade stands, bake sales, or school fundraisers for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers comparing savings options for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(C)). Students should understand different ways to save money and the advantages and disadvantages of each option. Key concepts: savings accounts earn interest on deposited money; different accounts may offer different interest rates; some accounts have minimum balance requirements or withdrawal limits; saving money helps prepare for future needs and emergencies; the concept of interest means money can grow over time when saved in a bank. Vocabulary: savings account, interest, interest rate, deposit, withdrawal, minimum balance, bank, credit union, principal, compound interest, financial institution. Students should compare different savings options and make informed decisions about where to save. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Personal financial literacy.skill: Budget a weekly allowance: word problems
Maths - Personal financial literacy.prompt: This topic covers budgeting for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(D)). Students should understand how to create and manage a simple budget by planning income and expenses. Key concepts: a budget is a plan for how to spend and save money; income is the money received (allowance, earnings); expenses are the money spent; a balanced budget means expenses do not exceed income; budgets help people make decisions about spending priorities and saving for goals. Students should practice creating budgets with given incomes and expense categories, making choices about spending, and adjusting budgets when income or expenses change. Vocabulary: budget, income, expense, savings, spending, needs, wants, allowance, plan, balance, prioritize, adjust, goal. Problems should involve realistic budgeting scenarios appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers financial institution vocabulary for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(E)). Students should understand the basic functions and terminology associated with financial institutions like banks and credit unions. Key concepts: banks and credit unions are places where people can safely store money, borrow money, and earn interest; key services include checking accounts, savings accounts, and loans; deposits add money to an account, withdrawals take money out; interest is money paid by the bank on savings or charged on loans. Vocabulary: bank, credit union, savings account, checking account, deposit, withdrawal, interest, loan, balance, statement, ATM, online banking, financial institution, account. Students should understand why people use financial institutions and the basic services they provide. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Miscellaneous.prompt: This topic covers place value to millions for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.2(B)). Students should understand the structure of the place value system extending to millions. Key concepts: the ones period contains ones, tens, hundreds; the thousands period contains thousands, ten thousands, hundred thousands; the millions period contains millions, ten millions, hundred millions; each period groups three digits; commas separate periods; students should read, write, and represent numbers to millions in standard, expanded, and word form. Vocabulary: place value, millions, period, digit, standard form, expanded form, word form, comma, compare, order. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers multi-digit multiplication for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.4(D)). Students should understand how to multiply numbers with multiple digits using efficient strategies. Key concepts: the standard algorithm, partial products, area models, and lattice multiplication are all valid strategies; place value understanding is essential for aligning partial products correctly; multiplication properties (commutative, associative, distributive) can simplify computation; estimating products helps check reasonableness. Vocabulary: multiply, product, factor, algorithm, partial product, area model, lattice, place value, regroup, estimate, commutative, distributive. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers division strategies for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.4(F)). Students should understand multiple approaches to division problems. Key concepts: standard long division algorithm (divide, multiply, subtract, bring down); partial quotients method (subtract convenient multiples of the divisor); using compatible numbers for estimation; breaking the dividend into friendlier parts using the distributive property; repeated subtraction as a conceptual foundation. Vocabulary: division, strategy, algorithm, partial quotient, compatible numbers, estimate, distributive property, repeated subtraction, dividend, divisor, quotient, remainder. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers factors and multiples for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.4(D)). Students should understand the relationship between factors, multiples, and divisibility. Key concepts: a factor of a number divides it evenly (no remainder); a multiple of a number is the product of that number and any whole number; factor pairs multiply to give the original number; the GCF (greatest common factor) is the largest factor shared by two numbers; the LCM (least common multiple) is the smallest multiple shared by two numbers; divisibility rules help identify factors quickly. Vocabulary: factor, multiple, divisible, GCF, LCM, factor pair, product, remainder, divisibility rule, prime, composite. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers rounding numbers for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.2(D)). Students should understand how to round whole numbers and decimals to a specified place value. Key concepts: identify the digit in the rounding place; look at the digit to its right (the 'decision digit'); if the decision digit is 5 or greater, round up; if less than 5, round down (keep the same); all digits to the right of the rounding place become zeros (for whole numbers) or are dropped (for decimals); rounding is used for estimation. Vocabulary: round, place value, estimate, digit, round up, round down, nearest, approximately, rounding place, decision digit. Common errors include rounding to the wrong place or changing digits to the left. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers addition and subtraction with regrouping for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.4(A)). Students should master the standard algorithms for multi-digit addition and subtraction that require regrouping (carrying or borrowing). Key concepts: in addition, when the sum of digits in any column exceeds 9, regroup by carrying to the next place; in subtraction, when the top digit is smaller than the bottom digit, regroup by borrowing from the next higher place; proper place value alignment is essential; estimate before computing to check reasonableness. Vocabulary: regroup, carry, borrow, addition, subtraction, place value, align, digit, sum, difference, algorithm, exchange. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers adding and subtracting fractions for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.3(E)). Students should understand how to add and subtract fractions with like denominators and begin exploring unlike denominators. Key concepts: with like denominators, add or subtract numerators and keep the denominator; simplify results when possible; use visual models (area models, number lines, strip diagrams) to support understanding; mixed numbers may require regrouping; estimate sums and differences using benchmark fractions. Vocabulary: fraction, add, subtract, numerator, denominator, like denominator, simplify, mixed number, regroup, benchmark, model, sum, difference. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers comparing decimals and fractions for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.3(D)). Students should understand how to compare numbers expressed in different forms (fractions and decimals). Key concepts: convert all values to the same form before comparing; fractions can be converted to decimals by dividing numerator by denominator; decimals can be converted to fractions using place value; common benchmarks (1/2 = 0.5, 1/4 = 0.25) help with quick comparisons; use <, >, or = to express the relationship. Vocabulary: fraction, decimal, compare, convert, equivalent, benchmark, greater than, less than, equal, numerator, denominator, place value. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers equivalent fractions for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.3(C)). Students should understand that equivalent fractions represent the same value using different numerators and denominators. Key concepts: equivalent fractions can be found by multiplying or dividing both the numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number; visual models (area models, number lines) can demonstrate equivalence; fractions are in simplest form (lowest terms) when the GCF of numerator and denominator is 1; students should generate and identify equivalent fractions. Vocabulary: equivalent, fraction, numerator, denominator, multiply, divide, simplify, lowest terms, GCF, model, equal, same value. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers introduction to decimals for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.2(E)). Students should build foundational understanding of decimal notation and meaning. Key concepts: a decimal is a way to represent numbers between whole numbers; the decimal point separates the whole number part from the fractional part; tenths are the first place to the right of the decimal point, hundredths are the second; decimals connect to fractions (0.5 = 5/10 = 1/2); decimals are used in money, measurement, and many real-world contexts. Vocabulary: decimal, decimal point, tenths, hundredths, thousandths, fraction, whole number, place value, notation, represent. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers geometric measurements including area and perimeter for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.5(D)). Students should understand the difference between area (space inside a figure) and perimeter (distance around a figure). Key concepts: perimeter = sum of all sides; area of a rectangle = length x width; perimeter is measured in linear units, area in square units; rectangles with the same area can have different perimeters and vice versa; students should solve problems involving both measurements. Vocabulary: area, perimeter, length, width, side, square units, linear units, rectangle, formula, measurement, space, distance, calculate. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers lines and angles for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(A)). Students should understand the basic geometric concepts of points, lines, line segments, rays, and angles. Key concepts: a line extends infinitely in both directions; a line segment has two endpoints; a ray has one endpoint; parallel lines never intersect; perpendicular lines meet at right angles; angles are classified as acute (less than 90 degrees), right (exactly 90 degrees), obtuse (between 90 and 180 degrees), and straight (180 degrees). Vocabulary: point, line, line segment, ray, angle, parallel, perpendicular, intersecting, acute, right, obtuse, straight, vertex, degree. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Miscellaneous.skill: Data representation in tables and graphs
Maths - Miscellaneous.prompt: This topic covers data representation and graphing for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(A)). Students should understand how to organize, display, and interpret data using various types of graphs and tables. Key concepts: different types of displays include bar graphs, line plots, pictographs, tables, and frequency charts; choose the display that best represents the data type; all displays need titles, labels, and appropriate scales; students should create displays from data sets and interpret existing displays to answer questions. Vocabulary: data, graph, table, bar graph, line plot, frequency, title, label, scale, axis, interpret, display, organize, represent. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers analyzing data from various representations for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(B)). Students should understand how to interpret data displays to answer questions and draw conclusions. Key concepts: read and interpret line plots, frequency charts, stem-and-leaf plots, and tables; identify the most and least common values; calculate totals and differences from data; answer multi-step questions requiring computation with data values; describe patterns and trends in data. Vocabulary: data, analyze, interpret, line plot, frequency, table, pattern, trend, most common, least common, total, compare, conclusion. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Maths - Miscellaneous.skill: Solving word problems with multiple steps
Maths - Miscellaneous.prompt: This topic covers solving word problems for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.5(A)). Students should understand how to analyze word problems to determine the correct operation(s) and solve them. Key concepts: read carefully to understand what is being asked; identify the given information and what needs to be found; determine which operation(s) to use based on the context; solve and check that the answer is reasonable; multi-step problems may require more than one operation. Vocabulary: word problem, operation, solve, strategy, reasonable, estimate, equation, unknown, given, question, steps, interpret, check. Problems should involve real-world contexts appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers multi-digit multiplication for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.4(D)). Students should understand how to multiply numbers with multiple digits using efficient strategies. Key concepts: the standard algorithm, partial products, area models, and lattice multiplication are all valid strategies; place value understanding is essential for aligning partial products correctly; multiplication properties (commutative, associative, distributive) can simplify computation; estimating products helps check reasonableness. Vocabulary: multiply, product, factor, algorithm, partial product, area model, lattice, place value, regroup, estimate, commutative, distributive. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers introduction to decimals for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.2(E)). Students should build foundational understanding of decimal notation and meaning. Key concepts: a decimal is a way to represent numbers between whole numbers; the decimal point separates the whole number part from the fractional part; tenths are the first place to the right of the decimal point, hundredths are the second; decimals connect to fractions (0.5 = 5/10 = 1/2); decimals are used in money, measurement, and many real-world contexts. Vocabulary: decimal, decimal point, tenths, hundredths, thousandths, fraction, whole number, place value, notation, represent. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers creating number sentences and equations for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.5(A)). Students should understand how to translate word problems and real-world situations into mathematical equations. Key concepts: identify the unknown and represent it with a symbol or variable; determine the operations from the context; write an equation that models the situation; use key vocabulary to identify operations (sum for addition, difference for subtraction, product for multiplication, quotient for division). Vocabulary: equation, number sentence, variable, unknown, operation, translate, model, represent, solve, equal sign, expression, symbol. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers perimeter and area of shapes for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.5(D)). Students should understand how to calculate both measurements for rectangles and composite figures. Key concepts: perimeter is found by adding all side lengths; area of a rectangle = length x width; for composite figures, decompose into rectangles, find each area, and add; students should solve word problems involving fencing (perimeter) and covering (area); understand the units differ (linear vs. square). Vocabulary: perimeter, area, length, width, side, rectangle, composite figure, decompose, square units, linear units, formula, measure, calculate. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

This topic covers data representation and graphing for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(A)). Students should understand how to organize, display, and interpret data using various types of graphs and tables. Key concepts: different types of displays include bar graphs, line plots, pictographs, tables, and frequency charts; choose the display that best represents the data type; all displays need titles, labels, and appropriate scales; students should create displays from data sets and interpret existing displays to answer questions. Vocabulary: data, graph, table, bar graph, line plot, frequency, title, label, scale, axis, interpret, display, organize, represent. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Classify and describe matter using observable properties
Science.prompt: This topic covers the states of matter (solid, liquid, gas) for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(A)). The TEKS standard states: Classify and describe matter using observable properties. Students should understand that matter exists in three common states and that each state has distinct observable properties. Key concepts: solids have a definite shape and volume, liquids have a definite volume but take the shape of their container, gases have neither definite shape nor volume and expand to fill their container. Students should understand that temperature changes can cause matter to change state (melting, freezing, evaporation, condensation). Vocabulary: matter, solid, liquid, gas, properties, volume, shape, temperature, melting point, boiling point, freezing point, evaporation, condensation. Students should be able to classify everyday materials by their state and describe the particle arrangement in each state. Activities should involve observing and comparing properties of different states and predicting state changes. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds and connect to real-world examples like ice melting, water boiling, and air filling a balloon.

Science.teks_description: Classify and describe matter using observable properties
Science.prompt: This topic covers properties of matter for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(A)). The TEKS standard states: Classify and describe matter using observable properties. Students should understand that matter can be described and classified using observable and measurable physical properties. Key concepts: mass, volume, density, color, texture, hardness, flexibility, magnetism, solubility, and conductivity. Students should distinguish between physical properties that can be observed (color, shape, texture) and those that must be measured (mass, volume, temperature). They should understand that physical properties can be used to identify, classify, and compare different substances. Vocabulary: matter, property, mass, volume, density, texture, hardness, magnetism, solubility, conductivity, classify, observe, measure. Students should practice describing substances using multiple properties and sorting materials into groups based on shared characteristics. Problems should include hands-on observation scenarios and comparison tasks appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Demonstrate matter conservation
Science.prompt: This topic covers matter and mass conservation for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(C)). The TEKS standard states: Demonstrate matter conservation. Students should understand the law of conservation of matter: matter is neither created nor destroyed in ordinary physical and chemical changes. Key concepts: mass remains constant when matter changes form (e.g., ice melting into water), mass is conserved in chemical reactions (e.g., combining baking soda and vinegar), and the total mass of a closed system does not change. Students should be able to predict and verify that the mass before and after a change remains the same using a balance. Vocabulary: matter, mass, conservation, physical change, chemical change, balance, closed system, reaction. Activities should involve measuring mass before and after changes. Problems should be grade-appropriate for 9-10 year olds and include real-world examples.

Science.teks_description: Investigate energy transfer
Science.prompt: This topic covers heat and thermal energy for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.8(A)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate energy transfer. Students should understand that thermal energy (heat) is the energy of moving particles in matter and that heat transfers from warmer objects to cooler objects. Key concepts: conduction (heat transfer through direct contact), convection (heat transfer through fluid movement), radiation (heat transfer through electromagnetic waves), and insulators versus conductors. Students should understand that temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles. Vocabulary: thermal energy, heat, temperature, conduction, convection, radiation, conductor, insulator, kinetic energy, transfer, thermometer. Students should explore real-world examples like metal spoons heating up in soup (conduction), warm air rising (convection), and sunshine warming skin (radiation). Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Investigate and compare mixtures
Science.prompt: This topic covers physical and chemical changes for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(B)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate and compare mixtures. Students should understand the difference between physical changes (changes in appearance without forming new substances) and chemical changes (changes that produce new substances with different properties). Key concepts: physical changes include cutting, melting, dissolving, and changes of state; chemical changes include burning, rusting, cooking, and combining substances that produce gas, color change, or temperature change. Signs of chemical change: gas production, color change, temperature change, new odor, precipitate formation. Vocabulary: physical change, chemical change, reversible, irreversible, substance, reaction, evidence, property. Students should be able to identify whether a change is physical or chemical and cite evidence. Problems should include everyday scenarios appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Investigate and compare mixtures
Science.prompt: This topic covers mixtures and solutions for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.6(B)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate and compare mixtures. Students should understand that a mixture is a combination of two or more substances that are not chemically combined and retain their individual properties. Key concepts: mixtures can be separated by physical means (filtering, evaporation, magnetism, sifting); solutions are a special type of mixture where one substance dissolves in another; the substance that dissolves is the solute, the substance doing the dissolving is the solvent. Students should understand the difference between heterogeneous mixtures (visibly different parts) and homogeneous mixtures (solutions that look uniform). Vocabulary: mixture, solution, solute, solvent, dissolve, filter, evaporate, separate, heterogeneous, homogeneous, concentration. Problems should involve identifying mixtures and solutions in everyday life, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Model slow changes from weathering and erosion
Science.prompt: This topic covers erosion for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(B)). The TEKS standard states: Model slow changes from weathering and erosion. Students should understand that erosion is the process by which weathered rock and soil are moved from one location to another by natural forces. Key concepts: agents of erosion include water (rivers, waves, rain), wind, ice (glaciers), and gravity; weathering breaks rocks into smaller pieces while erosion transports those pieces; deposition occurs when eroded material is dropped in a new location. Students should understand slow versus rapid changes to Earth's surface and how human activities can increase or decrease erosion. Vocabulary: erosion, weathering, deposition, sediment, glacier, landslide, delta, canyon, sand dune, prevention, conservation. Problems should include real-world examples and modeling activities appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Investigate energy transfer
Science.prompt: This topic covers waves and their properties for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.8(A)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate energy transfer. Students should understand that waves transfer energy from one place to another without transferring matter. Key concepts: waves have measurable properties including wavelength (distance between wave peaks), amplitude (height of wave), and frequency (number of waves per second); there are two main types of waves: transverse (particles move perpendicular to wave direction) and longitudinal (particles move parallel to wave direction). Students should understand that sound travels as longitudinal waves and light travels as transverse waves. Vocabulary: wave, energy, wavelength, amplitude, frequency, crest, trough, transverse, longitudinal, medium, vibration. Problems should involve identifying wave properties and comparing different waves, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Explore how plant/organism structures enable survival
Science.prompt: This topic covers human body systems for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.13(A)). The TEKS standard states: Explore how plant/organism structures enable survival. Students should understand that the human body is organized into systems that work together to carry out life functions. Key concepts: major body systems include skeletal (support and protection), muscular (movement), digestive (breaking down food), circulatory (transporting blood, oxygen, nutrients), respiratory (gas exchange), nervous (sending signals), and excretory (removing waste). Students should understand how structures within each system are specialized for their function and how systems interact with each other. Vocabulary: organ, tissue, system, function, structure, skeleton, muscle, digestion, circulation, respiration, nerve, cell. Students should connect body system functions to everyday experiences like breathing, eating, and moving. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Investigate energy transfer
Science.prompt: This topic covers light and energy transfer for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.8(A)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate energy transfer. Students should understand that light is a form of energy that travels in straight lines and can be reflected, refracted, or absorbed by different materials. Key concepts: transparent materials allow light to pass through, translucent materials allow some light through, opaque materials block light; reflection occurs when light bounces off a surface; refraction occurs when light bends as it passes from one material to another; absorption occurs when materials take in light energy. Vocabulary: light, energy, reflection, refraction, absorption, transparent, translucent, opaque, shadow, lens, prism, spectrum. Students should explore how light interacts with various materials and understand that the color of an object depends on which wavelengths of light it reflects. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Demonstrate electrical energy in closed paths
Science.prompt: This topic covers electricity and magnetism for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.8(C)). The TEKS standard states: Demonstrate electrical energy in closed paths. Students should understand that electrical energy flows through closed circuits and that magnetism is a force that can attract or repel certain materials. Key concepts: closed versus open circuits, conductors (materials that allow electricity to flow) versus insulators (materials that block electricity), series versus parallel circuits, magnets have north and south poles, like poles repel and opposite poles attract, electromagnets are created when electricity flows through a coil of wire around a metal core. Vocabulary: circuit, conductor, insulator, current, battery, switch, series circuit, parallel circuit, magnet, magnetic field, poles, attract, repel, electromagnet. Students should understand practical applications of circuits and magnets. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Explore how plant structures enable survival
Science.prompt: This topic covers structure and function in organisms for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.13(A)). The TEKS standard states: Explore how plant structures enable survival. Students should understand that plants and animals have specialized structures that serve specific functions for survival. Key concepts: plant structures include roots (absorb water and nutrients, anchor plant), stems (transport water and nutrients, provide support), leaves (photosynthesis, gas exchange), and flowers/seeds (reproduction); animal structures include eyes, ears, claws, beaks, and camouflage patterns that help with survival. Students should understand the relationship between a structure and its function, and how these adaptations help organisms survive in their environments. Vocabulary: structure, function, adaptation, survival, root, stem, leaf, flower, seed, organ, tissue. Problems should involve identifying structures and explaining how they help organisms survive, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe water cycle movement
Science.prompt: This topic covers the water cycle for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.10(A)). The TEKS standard states: Describe water cycle movement. Students should understand that water continuously moves through Earth's systems in a cycle driven by the Sun's energy. Key concepts: evaporation (liquid water becomes water vapor due to heat), condensation (water vapor cools and forms clouds), precipitation (water falls as rain, snow, sleet, or hail), collection/runoff (water gathers in oceans, lakes, rivers, and groundwater). Students should understand that the Sun provides the energy that drives the water cycle and that the total amount of water on Earth remains relatively constant. Vocabulary: water cycle, evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, groundwater, collection, water vapor, clouds, transpiration, aquifer. Students should be able to model and describe each stage of the cycle. Problems should connect to real-world weather observations appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.skill: The solar system and movement of Earth
Science.teks_description: Collect data on seasonal patterns (Earth-Sun relationship)
Science.prompt: This topic covers the solar system and movement of Earth for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.9(A)). The TEKS standard states: Collect data on seasonal patterns (Earth-Sun relationship). Students should understand that Earth rotates on its axis causing day and night and revolves around the Sun causing seasons. Key concepts: Earth's rotation takes approximately 24 hours (one day); Earth's revolution around the Sun takes approximately 365.25 days (one year); Earth's tilted axis (23.5 degrees) causes seasonal changes as different parts of Earth receive varying amounts of direct sunlight throughout the year. Students should understand the order of planets from the Sun and basic characteristics of inner versus outer planets. Vocabulary: rotation, revolution, axis, tilt, orbit, season, planet, Sun, solar system, inner planets, outer planets. Problems should include predicting seasonal patterns and understanding day/night cycles, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Investigate how producers make food
Science.prompt: This topic covers photosynthesis for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.12(A)). The TEKS standard states: Investigate how producers make food. Students should understand that photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce food (glucose) and release oxygen. Key concepts: plants are producers because they make their own food; photosynthesis occurs primarily in leaves using chlorophyll (the green pigment); the equation for photosynthesis involves sunlight + water + carbon dioxide producing glucose + oxygen; plants need sunlight, water, carbon dioxide, and nutrients from soil to grow. Students should understand that photosynthesis is the foundation of most food chains because it converts light energy into chemical energy stored in food. Vocabulary: photosynthesis, chlorophyll, producer, glucose, carbon dioxide, oxygen, sunlight, chloroplast, energy, food chain. Problems should involve tracing energy flow and explaining what plants need to make food, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Determine rock properties for resource storage
Science.prompt: This topic covers rock formations for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.11(C)). The TEKS standard states: Determine rock properties for resource storage. Students should understand that rocks are classified into three types based on how they form. Key concepts: igneous rocks form from cooled magma or lava (e.g., granite, obsidian); sedimentary rocks form from layers of sediment compressed over time (e.g., sandstone, limestone); metamorphic rocks form when existing rocks are changed by heat and pressure (e.g., marble, slate). The rock cycle describes how rocks continuously change from one type to another over long periods. Students should understand that rocks have different properties (texture, color, hardness, layers) based on how they formed. Vocabulary: igneous, sedimentary, metamorphic, magma, lava, sediment, weathering, erosion, rock cycle, mineral, crystal, fossil. Problems should include identifying rock types and describing formation processes, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Describe matter cycling and energy flow in food webs
Science.prompt: This topic covers aquatic biomes for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.12(B)). The TEKS standard states: Describe matter cycling and energy flow in food webs. Students should understand that aquatic biomes are large regions of water characterized by distinct environmental conditions and organisms. Key concepts: freshwater biomes include lakes, ponds, rivers, and streams; marine biomes include oceans, coral reefs, and estuaries; organisms in aquatic biomes are adapted to their specific water conditions (salinity, temperature, depth, light). Students should understand food webs in aquatic environments and how energy flows from producers (algae, aquatic plants) to consumers. Vocabulary: biome, freshwater, marine, estuary, salinity, coral reef, plankton, algae, food web, producer, consumer, decomposer, ecosystem, habitat. Students should compare different aquatic environments and the organisms that live in them. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Use scientific/engineering practices for investigations
Science.prompt: This topic covers scientific investigations and the scientific method for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.1(B)). The TEKS standard states: Use scientific/engineering practices for investigations. Students should understand that scientific investigations follow systematic processes to explore questions about the natural world. Key concepts: asking testable questions, forming hypotheses, designing fair tests with controlled variables, collecting and recording data, analyzing results, and drawing evidence-based conclusions. Students should understand the difference between independent variables (what you change), dependent variables (what you measure), and controlled variables (what you keep the same). They should also understand the importance of repeating trials for reliability. Vocabulary: hypothesis, variable, independent variable, dependent variable, controlled variable, data, evidence, conclusion, experiment, observation, prediction, fair test, trial, reliability. Problems should involve designing experiments and identifying variables, appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.skill: Representing data in graphs and tables
Science.teks_description: Construct graphic organizers for data collection
Science.prompt: This topic covers representing data in graphs and tables for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.1(F)). The TEKS standard states: Construct graphic organizers for data collection. Students should understand how to organize, display, and interpret scientific data using various formats. Key concepts: data tables organize raw observations and measurements in rows and columns; bar graphs compare categories of data; line graphs show changes over time; pictographs use symbols to represent quantities; circle graphs show parts of a whole. Students should choose the most appropriate graph type for different kinds of data and be able to draw conclusions from data displays. Vocabulary: data, table, graph, bar graph, line graph, pictograph, circle graph, axis, label, title, scale, trend, conclusion, variable. Students should practice creating graphs from collected data and interpreting graphs made by others. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.

Science.teks_description: Collect observations and measurements as evidence
Science.prompt: This topic covers using science notebooks for Grade 4 students (TEKS 4.1(E)). The TEKS standard states: Collect observations and measurements as evidence. Students should understand that science notebooks are essential tools for recording observations, measurements, data, and reflections during scientific investigations. Key concepts: recording detailed observations using words, numbers, and drawings; organizing data in tables and charts; labeling diagrams accurately; writing procedures so investigations can be repeated; recording measurements with appropriate units; reflecting on results and drawing conclusions from evidence. Students should understand that good scientific records are detailed, accurate, organized, and dated. Vocabulary: observation, measurement, data, evidence, record, diagram, procedure, conclusion, units, accuracy, precision, organize. Students should practice maintaining organized notebooks throughout investigations. Problems should be appropriate for 9-10 year olds.
